annotations.label: Affine algebraic plane curve

anchor.snippet: an affine algebraic plane curve is the zero set of a polynomial in two variables

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the affine zero locus of polynomials (any number of variables) but has no dedicated plane-curve type.

annotations.label: Projective algebraic plane curve

anchor.snippet: A projective algebraic plane curve is the zero set in a projective plane of a homogeneous polynomial in three variables

annotations.note: The projective zero locus of homogeneous elements is formalized, but not the specific notion of a plane projective curve.

annotations.label: Irreducible plane algebraic curve

anchor.snippet: If the defining polynomial of a plane algebraic curve is irreducible

annotations.note: Irreducibility of the defining polynomial is captured by the general `Irreducible` predicate, but irreducible curves are not a defined object.

annotations.label: Components of a curve

anchor.snippet: called its components

annotations.note: Topological irreducible components are formalized generally, but not specialized to the components of an algebraic curve.

annotations.label: Algebraic curve

anchor.snippet: More generally, an algebraic curve is an algebraic variety of dimension one

annotations.note: Mathlib has schemes and Krull dimension but no definition of an algebraic curve as a one-dimensional variety.

annotations.label: Algebraic curve via birational equivalence

anchor.snippet: an algebraic curve is an algebraic variety that is birationally equivalent to an irreducible algebraic plane curve

annotations.note: Rational maps of schemes are formalized so birational equivalence is expressible, but this characterization of curves is not.

annotations.label: Space curve

anchor.snippet: A non-plane curve is often called a space curve or a skew curve

annotations.note: No notion of space/skew curve is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Algebraic curve in the Euclidean plane

anchor.snippet: An algebraic curve in the Euclidean plane is the set of the points

annotations.note: The real solution set is an instance of the general affine zero locus, but real plane curves are not a defined object.

annotations.label: Decomposition into monotone arcs

anchor.snippet: Every algebraic curve may be uniquely decomposed into a finite number of smooth monotone arcs

annotations.note: No decomposition of real algebraic curves into monotone arcs is formalized.

annotations.label: Smooth monotone arc (branch)

anchor.snippet: A smooth monotone arc is the graph of a smooth function

annotations.note: The notion of a smooth monotone arc of a curve is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tschirnhausen cubic

anchor.snippet: for the Tschirnhausen cubic , there are two infinite arcs having the origin

annotations.note: The Tschirnhausen cubic is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sinusoid is not algebraic

anchor.snippet: the sinusoid is certainly not an algebraic curve

annotations.note: No result that the sine graph is non-algebraic is formalized.

annotations.label: Plane projective curve

anchor.snippet: An algebraic curve in the projective plane or plane projective curve is the set of the points

annotations.note: Projective zero loci are formalized, but not the specific notion of a plane projective curve.

annotations.label: Projective completion

anchor.snippet: may be completed into the projective curve

annotations.note: Projective completion (homogenization) of a plane curve is not formalized as such.

anchors.snippet: may be completed into the projective curve

annotations.label: Projective completion of unit circle

anchor.snippet: is the projective completion of the unit circle

annotations.note: This specific projective completion example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Derivative at infinity

anchor.snippet: it is useful to consider the derivative at infinity

annotations.note: No notion of derivative at the line at infinity for plane curves is formalized.

anchors.snippet: it is useful to consider the derivative at infinity

annotations.label: Line meets curve in at most d points

anchor.snippet: any line cuts the curve in at most d points

annotations.note: The underlying fact that a degree-d polynomial has at most d roots is formalized, but not its geometric application to line–curve intersection.

annotations.label: Bézout's theorem

anchor.snippet: Bézout's theorem asserts that this number is exactly d

annotations.note: Bézout's theorem on intersection of plane curves is not formalized (tracked but undeclared in docs/1000.yaml).

annotations.label: Multiplicity of an intersection point

anchor.snippet: The multiplicity of an intersection point is the multiplicity of the corresponding root

annotations.note: Root multiplicity of a polynomial is formalized, which is the algebraic content, but intersection multiplicity of curves is not defined.

annotations.label: Intersection point at infinity

anchor.snippet: There is an intersection point at infinity if the degree of q is lower than the degree of p

annotations.note: Intersection points at infinity for plane curves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Tangent at a point

anchor.snippet: The tangent at a point ( a , b ) of the curve is the line of equation

annotations.note: The tangent line of a plane algebraic curve via partial derivatives is not formalized.

annotations.label: Singular point (undefined tangent)

anchor.snippet: the tangent is not defined and the point is a singular point

annotations.note: Singular/nonsingular points (vanishing of both partial derivatives) are defined only for the special case of Weierstrass curves.

annotations.label: Tangent in projective case

anchor.snippet: The equation of the tangent of at the point of projective coordinates ( a : b : c )

annotations.note: The projective tangent line equation for plane curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Infinite branch and point at infinity

anchor.snippet: Every infinite branch of an algebraic curve corresponds to a point at infinity

annotations.note: No correspondence between infinite branches and points at infinity is formalized.

annotations.label: Asymptote

anchor.snippet: The corresponding asymptote is the tangent of the curve at that point

annotations.note: Asymptotes of algebraic curves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Asymptotic direction

anchor.snippet: one says that ( a , b ) is an asymptotic direction

annotations.note: Asymptotic directions are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: one says that ( a , b ) is an asymptotic direction

annotations.label: Parabolic branch

anchor.snippet: one says that the curve has a parabolic branch

annotations.note: Parabolic branches are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: one says that the curve has a parabolic branch

annotations.label: Singular points system

anchor.snippet: The singular points of a curve of degree d defined by a polynomial

annotations.note: The simultaneous vanishing of partial derivatives defines singularity only for Weierstrass curves, not general plane curves of degree d.

anchors.snippet: The singular points of a curve of degree d defined by a polynomial

annotations.label: Bound (d−1)² on singular points

anchor.snippet: the number of singular points is at most ( d − 1) 2

annotations.note: No bound on the number of singular points of a plane curve is formalized.

annotations.label: Sharp bound d(d−1)/2

anchor.snippet: the sharp bound is d ( d − 1)/2

annotations.note: This sharp singular-point bound is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bound for irreducible curves

anchor.snippet: For irreducible curves and polynomials, the number of singular points is at most ( d − 1)( d − 2)/2

annotations.note: The (d−1)(d−2)/2 bound for irreducible curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tangents and multiplicity at singular point

anchor.snippet: The equation of the tangents at a singular point is given by the nonzero homogeneous part of the lowest degree

annotations.note: Tangent cone / multiplicity at a singular point via lowest homogeneous part is not formalized.

annotations.label: Branches near a singular point

anchor.snippet: near a singular point, a real algebraic curve is the union of a finite number of branches

annotations.note: The local branch decomposition near a singular point is not formalized.

annotations.label: Coordinate analytic near regular point

anchor.snippet: Near a regular point, one of the coordinates of the curve may be expressed as an analytic function

annotations.note: The implicit function theorem is formalized, but the real-analytic version giving local analytic parameterization of a curve is not stated.

annotations.label: Puiseux factorization

anchor.snippet: Thus f may be factored in factors of the form

annotations.note: Puiseux series and the Puiseux factorization theorem are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conjugate branch and ramification index

anchor.snippet: These d series are said conjugate

annotations.note: Conjugate Puiseux branches and their ramification index are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: These d series are said conjugate

annotations.label: Real curve cases

anchor.snippet: three cases may occur

annotations.note: The case analysis of real local branches is not formalized.

annotations.label: Ordinary cusp branch

anchor.snippet: the ordinary cusp has only one branch

annotations.note: The ordinary cusp and its branch structure are not formalized.

annotations.label: Affine curve needs n−1 polynomials

anchor.snippet: an affine curve in an affine space of dimension n is defined by, at least, n − 1 polynomials

annotations.note: No statement about the minimal number of defining polynomials for a curve in n-space is formalized.

annotations.label: Birational representation of non-plane curve

anchor.snippet: This representation is a birational equivalence between the curve and the plane curve

annotations.note: The birational projection of a space curve to a plane curve is not formalized.

annotations.label: Curves and function fields equivalence

anchor.snippet: the irreducible curves over a field F are categorically equivalent to algebraic function fields in one variable over F

annotations.note: The categorical (anti)equivalence between curves and function fields is not formalized.

annotations.label: Algebraic function field

anchor.snippet: Such an algebraic function field is a field extension K of F that contains an element x which is transcendental over F

annotations.note: `FunctionField F K` is defined as K being a finite extension of the rational function field F(X), i.e. an algebraic function field in one variable.

annotations.label: Elliptic function field

anchor.snippet: then the field C ( x , y ) is an elliptic function field

annotations.note: The elliptic function field example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Curve with no real points

anchor.snippet: the corresponding curve considered as a subset of R 2 has no points

annotations.note: This example of a curve with no real points is not formalized.

annotations.label: Isomorphism of nonsingular curves

anchor.snippet: Two nonsingular projective curves over a field are isomorphic if and only if their function fields are isomorphic

annotations.note: The equivalence of curve isomorphism and function-field isomorphism is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tsen's theorem

anchor.snippet: Tsen's theorem is about the function field of an algebraic curve

annotations.note: Tsen's theorem is not formalized (tracked but undeclared in docs/1000.yaml).

annotations.label: Complex projective curve is compact, connected, orientable

anchor.snippet: is compact , connected , and orientable

annotations.note: No result that a smooth complex projective curve is a compact connected orientable surface is formalized.

annotations.label: Algebraic curve over C is a surface

anchor.snippet: An algebraic curve over C likewise has topological dimension two

annotations.note: The fact that a complex algebraic curve has real topological dimension two is not formalized.

annotations.label: Topological genus equals geometric genus

anchor.snippet: is equal to the geometric genus of the algebraic curve

annotations.note: Genus of a curve is not formalized in Mathlib, so this equality is not present.

annotations.label: Riemann surface

anchor.snippet: A Riemann surface is a connected complex analytic manifold of one complex dimension

annotations.note: Mathlib has complex manifolds but no dedicated Riemann surface definition.

annotations.label: Triple equivalence of categories

anchor.snippet: There is a triple equivalence of categories between the category of smooth irreducible projective algebraic curves

annotations.note: The triple equivalence (curves / compact Riemann surfaces / function fields) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Smooth vs singular point

anchor.snippet: points P on an algebraic curve C are classified as smooth

annotations.note: Smooth/singular point classification exists only for the special case of Weierstrass curves (general scheme smoothness is also available).

anchors.snippet: points P on an algebraic curve C are classified as smooth

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\frac {\partial f}{\partial x}}(P)={\frac {\partial f}{\partial y}}(P)={\frac {\partial f}{\partial z}}(P)=0.}

annotations.label: Singular points of plane projective curve

anchor.snippet: the singular points are precisely the points P where the rank of the 1×( n + 1) matrix is zero

annotations.note: The Jacobian/partial-derivative criterion for singularity is formalized only for Weierstrass curves, not general projective plane curves.

anchors.snippet: the singular points are precisely the points P where the rank of the 1×( n + 1) matrix is zero

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\frac {\partial f}{\partial x}}(P)={\frac {\partial f}{\partial y}}(P)={\frac {\partial f}{\partial z}}(P)=0.}

annotations.label: Singularities not birational invariants

anchor.snippet: The singularities of a curve are not birational invariants

annotations.note: This statement about birational non-invariance of singularities is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cusp x³=y²

anchor.snippet: the curve with equation x 3 = y 2 at (0,0)

annotations.note: The cuspidal cubic example is not formalized as a curve singularity.

annotations.label: Finitely many singular points

anchor.snippet: A curve C has at most a finite number of singular points

annotations.note: Finiteness of the singular locus of a curve is not formalized.

annotations.label: Smooth (non-singular) curve

anchor.snippet: If it has none, it can be called smooth or non-singular

annotations.note: Nonsingularity is defined for Weierstrass curves, but a general smooth-curve definition is absent.

annotations.label: Multiplicity

anchor.snippet: The multiplicity m is defined as the maximum integer

annotations.note: Multiplicity of a curve singularity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Delta invariant

anchor.snippet: a singular point has delta invariant δ if it concentrates δ ordinary double points at P

annotations.note: The delta invariant of a singularity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Milnor number

anchor.snippet: The Milnor number μ of a singularity is the degree of the mapping

annotations.note: The Milnor number of a singularity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Milnor–Jung formula

anchor.snippet: It is related to δ and r by the Milnor–Jung formula

annotations.note: The Milnor–Jung formula μ = 2δ − r + 1 is not formalized.

annotations.label: Branching number

anchor.snippet: the branching number r of P is the number of locally irreducible branches at P

annotations.note: The branching number of a singular point is not formalized.

annotations.label: Inequalities m≥r and singular iff m≥2

anchor.snippet: The multiplicity m is at least r

annotations.note: These inequalities relating multiplicity and branching number are not formalized.

annotations.label: Genus formula

anchor.snippet: It is called the genus formula

annotations.note: The genus formula for plane curves with singularities is not formalized (no genus in Mathlib).

annotations.label: Invariants of ordinary singularities

anchor.snippet: an ordinary cusp is a point with invariants

annotations.note: The tabulated invariants of ordinary singularities (cusp, node, etc.) are not formalized.

annotations.label: Rational curve

anchor.snippet: A rational curve , also called a unicursal curve, is any curve which is birationally equivalent to a line

annotations.note: Rational maps of schemes exist so birational equivalence to a line is expressible, but rational curves are not a defined notion.

annotations.label: Rational curve equals genus zero

anchor.snippet: If F is algebraically closed, this is equivalent to a curve of genus zero

annotations.note: The equivalence of rationality with genus zero is not formalized (no genus in Mathlib).

annotations.label: Rational normal curve

anchor.snippet: An example is the rational normal curve

annotations.note: The rational normal curve is not formalized.

annotations.label: Conic with rational point is rational

anchor.snippet: Any conic section defined over F with a rational point in F is a rational curve

annotations.note: The rationality of a conic with a rational point is not formalized.

annotations.label: Ellipse rational parameterization

anchor.snippet: consider the ellipse x 2 + xy + y 2 = 1, where (−1, 0) is a rational point

annotations.note: This rational parameterization of an ellipse is not formalized.

annotations.label: Rational plane curve

anchor.snippet: defining a rational plane curve of degree

annotations.note: Rational plane curves given by degree-d parameterizations are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: defining a rational plane curve of degree

annotations.label: Moduli space of stable curves

anchor.snippet: parametrizing all such stable curves

annotations.note: Moduli spaces of stable curves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Counting rational plane curves (Gromov–Witten)

anchor.snippet: This moduli space can be used to count the number

annotations.note: Gromov–Witten counts of rational plane curves are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: This moduli space can be used to count the number

annotations.label: Elliptic curve

anchor.snippet: An elliptic curve may be defined as any curve of genus one with a rational point

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes elliptic curves as Weierstrass curves with unit discriminant rather than via the genus-one-with-rational-point characterization.

anchors.snippet: An elliptic curve may be defined as any curve of genus one with a rational point

annotations.label: Weierstrass form

anchor.snippet: a linear change of coordinates allows putting

annotations.note: A variable change putting a curve into short Weierstrass form y² = x³ + a₄x + a₆ (char ≠ 2,3) is proved.

anchors.snippet: a linear change of coordinates allows putting

annotations.label: Group structure on elliptic curve

anchor.snippet: Elliptic curves carry the structure of an abelian group

annotations.note: The nonsingular points of a Weierstrass curve form an abelian group under the chord-tangent law.

annotations.label: Three points collinear iff sum zero

anchor.snippet: three points sum to zero in the group if and only if they are collinear

annotations.note: The group law is defined via the collinearity (chord-tangent) rule, but no explicit 'collinear iff sum zero' lemma was found.

annotations.label: Group isomorphic to C modulo lattice

anchor.snippet: the group is isomorphic to the additive group of the complex plane modulo the period lattice

annotations.note: Complex uniformization of elliptic curves (C/lattice) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Intersection of two quadrics

anchor.snippet: The intersection of two quadric surfaces is, in general, a nonsingular curve of genus one and degree four

annotations.note: This description of the intersection of two quadrics is not formalized.

annotations.label: Faltings' theorem

anchor.section: Curves of genus greater than one
anchor.snippet: by Faltings' theorem , can have only a finite number of rational points

annotations.note: Faltings' theorem (Mordell conjecture) is not formalized (tracked but undeclared in docs/1000.yaml).

annotations.label: Genus greater than one examples

anchor.section: Curves of genus greater than one
anchor.snippet: Examples are the hyperelliptic curves , the Klein quartic curve

annotations.note: Hyperelliptic and Klein quartic curves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Genus of projective plane curve

anchor.snippet: which can be computed using coherent sheaf cohomology

annotations.note: The genus of a projective plane curve via cohomology is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: which can be computed using coherent sheaf cohomology

annotations.label: Smooth genus curve

anchor.snippet: which is smooth since the differentials

annotations.note: This smooth genus-curve example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-example: union of rational curves

anchor.snippet: A non-example of a generic section is the curve

annotations.note: This non-example involving a union of rational curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Genus of curve in product of projective lines

anchor.section: Curves in product of projective lines
anchor.snippet: which can be checked using coherent sheaf cohomology

annotations.note: The genus of a curve in P¹×P¹ via cohomology is not formalized.

anchors.section: Curves in product of projective lines
anchors.snippet: which can be checked using coherent sheaf cohomology

annotations.label: Any genus constructible

anchor.section: Curves in product of projective lines
anchor.snippet: hence a curve of any genus can be constructed as a curve in

annotations.note: The constructibility of curves of arbitrary genus is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: hence a curve of any genus can be constructed as a curve in
